justification: Pass 3 specifically repairs Pass 1 by accepting the scenario’s literal world-governing premise, removing the invented chronology, and narrowing the remaining problem to the scope of `person` and self-invitation. It is not merely sycophantic to Pass 2, because it explicitly rejects one of Pass 2’s overextensions while preserving a coherent causal analysis of the 3:00 PM state.

cited_span_refs.excerpt: I now take the scenario literally. The rules are said to be `absolute`, and they explicitly govern who may `physically exist` in the room. So ordinary “etiquette doesn’t control ontology” objections were misplaced.

I stopped assuming a hidden chronology requirement. Nothing says invitations must be issued from inside the room, or that the host must already have been in the room before beginning to pour. A simultaneous state at 3:00 PM is not ruled out by the text.
I narrowed the real issue to the strongest unresolved ambiguity: whether rule 1 applies to the host as a `person`, and if so whether self-invitation is allowed or whether `host` is implicitly exempt.
One part of Pass 2 goes too far in the other direction: it suggests the scenario may still be open between “mere etiquette” and “constitutive/metaphysical” rules.
